title: Capturing the Temporal Dependence of Training Data Influence

reviews.summary: This paper tackles the mismatch between classical influence estimation methods (notably influence functions) and modern training regimes where the order of data and optimization trajectory matter. It formalizes trajectory-specific leave-one-out (TSLOO) influence and proposes data value embedding (DVEmb), a scalable approximation that compresses each training example’s trajectory-dependent effect into a vector so that influences on any test query can be computed via a dot product. The method derives an unrolled SGD estimator, introduces a Gauss-Newton-based backward recursion for efficient computation, leverages gradient decomposition and random projections for storage, and presents an influence checkpointing scheme for parallelization; it demonstrates strong fidelity on small-scale ground-truth LOO and offers actionable insights on temporal phases of data influence in LLM pretraining with efficiency advantages over the most scalable influence-function baseline (LoGRA).
strengths: Reformulates data attribution around TSLOO and designs a new embedding (DVEmb) that explicitly captures order-dependent training dynamics and interactions.
Derives a backward recursion under a generalized Gauss-Newton (GGN) approximation that enables efficient computation of per-example DVEmb without re-running training.
Proposes influence checkpointing to parallelize embedding computation and to analyze data value dynamics across checkpoints.
Integrates practical systems ideas (gradient decomposition, random projection, per-layer factorization) to make per-sample, trajectory-aware attribution feasible at scale.
Correlates DVEmb with ground-truth LOO on MNIST MLP under both single-epoch and all-epoch removals, outperforming standard influence functions.
Provides a detailed efficiency comparison with LoGRA on Pythia-410M over Pile (1%), showing substantially improved throughput and much lower peak memory for the embedding computation phase.

Demonstrates impactful insights into LLM pretraining (early/late high-influence phases) and validates them via selective application of online data selection with competitive perplexity at notably reduced compute.
Clearly motivates the limitations of permutation-invariant assumptions and why order-dependence matters in foundation-model training.
Presents an intuitive geometric view of DVEmb and its dot-product attribution with test gradients.
Provides understandable interpretations (e.g., interaction effects via gradient similarity) of the recursion and its implications.
Addresses a highly relevant and under-served regime (single/few-epoch, curriculum-driven training) where classical influence functions underperform or mislead.
Offers a reusable artifact (precomputed embeddings) that supports real-time attribution queries for data governance, auditing, and curation.
The temporal-influence findings inspire practical, compute-saving strategies for online data selection and potentially curriculum design.
weaknesses: Heavy reliance on multiple approximations: first-order linearization of unrolled dynamics, GGN, per-layer independence, and random projections. Error accumulation and robustness trade-offs are not fully characterized.
Method is derived for SGD; experiments suggest using SGD as a proxy for Adam, but the optimizer mismatch and its effect on fidelity are not rigorously analyzed.
The complexity O(BT p̃^2) can still be large for long runs and p̃ in the thousands; analysis would benefit from end-to-end runtime/throughput curves as a function of T, p̃, and K (checkpoints).

Fidelity evaluation is limited to small models; there is no medium-scale setting (e.g., ResNet on CIFAR/ImageNet, moderate-size Transformers) with partial ground-truth or semi-synthetic LOO to bridge the gap to LLM scale.
Lacks empirical comparisons with trajectory-aware baselines such as TracIn, TRAK, or Data-Models, which also leverage training-history signals and/or dot-product embeddings.

The data-selection experiments show promising perplexity improvements, but statistical significance, sensitivity to validation set choice, and robustness to different schedules/seeds are not reported.
The cost of collecting and storing per-sample gradient decompositions during training (I/O bandwidth, wall-clock slowdown, instrumentation burden) is under-specified.

Some key algorithmic elements (e.g., the construction and maintenance of M^(t_s), checkpointing reconstruction) are deferred to the appendix; presenting a concise main-text pseudocode would aid reproducibility.
Notation around the running matrix and projected dimensions could be tightened; dual reporting of peak GPU memory (64.6GB/0.84GB) is confusing without more context.

Limited empirical comparison to prior trajectory-based or embedding-based attribution methods (TracIn; TRAK; Data-Models). A head-to-head study would contextualize the gains from the Hessian-propagation idea versus summation-based or kernel-based approaches.
Recent unrolling-based methods (e.g., Bae et al., 2024) are cited but not evaluated against; even small/medium-scale comparisons would strengthen the case.
reviews.questions:
1. How sensitive is DVEmb to optimizer mismatch? Can you report fidelity (e.g., correlations to ground-truth or proxy metrics) when the model is trained with Adam but DVEmb is computed assuming SGD, versus training and computing both with SGD?
2. What is the end-to-end training-time overhead of collecting per-sample gradient decompositions and projections (I/O, wall-clock slowdown), and how does it scale with batch size and sequence length for Transformers?
3. Can you provide head-to-head comparisons with TracIn, TRAK, and Data-Models on a medium-scale setting, including runtime, storage, and fidelity to LOO (or proxy tasks like mislabel detection and data selection)?
4. The complexity is O(BT p̃^2) (or per-layer reduced). For your Pythia-410M run (1% Pile), what are T, B, p̃, L, and K (checkpoints), and what are the measured flop counts and wall-clock times per phase? How does throughput vary with p̃?
5. How robust are the training-dynamics conclusions (early/late high impact) to changes in the learning rate schedule (warmup length, decay shape), tokenization, batch size, and data shuffling seeds?
6. Could you clarify the memory numbers in Table 1 (64.6GB/0.84GB*) and precisely which phase each corresponds to? Also, what fraction of the ~171GB storage is due to embeddings vs. auxiliary state?
7. What is the empirical effect of projection dimension and per-layer independence on the LLM downstream analyses (e.g., correlation with proxy metrics, selection effectiveness)? Any observed failure modes?
8. Does influence checkpointing introduce approximation error relative to the full backward pass? If so, how does this error scale with K, and what is the practical trade-off?
reviews.assessment: This paper addresses an important shortcoming of classical attribution methods in modern, order-sensitive training, and introduces a well-motivated, scalable alternative. The proposed data value embedding is technically novel in how it compresses trajectory-specific effects into reusable vectors and is supported by a thoughtful blend of approximations that make large-scale deployment plausible. The empirical evidence is promising, particularly the LOO fidelity at small scale, the efficiency gains over LoGRA, and the actionable insights about temporal phases of data influence that translate into compute-saving data selection strategies. However, the paper would be strengthened by broader fidelity evaluations (beyond MNIST), direct comparisons with trajectory-aware baselines (TracIn/TRAK/Data-Models), clearer reporting of instrumentation overhead, and more comprehensive robustness analyses (optimizer mismatch, schedules, seeds). Despite these gaps, the core idea is compelling, the methodology is sound under reasonable assumptions, and the potential value to the community is high. I lean toward acceptance, contingent on addressing the experimental comparisons and clarifying practical overheads.
